hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'me' — 'Ego' is nominative, the subject

steps.reveal: hoc animo = with this spirit/resolve (abl. of quality/description)

hints.nominative: Nominative neuter would be 'hoc' but 'animo' is ablative — the phrase is ablative of description
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'hunc animum' — 'hoc animo' with -o endings is ablative
hints.dative: Dative would be 'huic animo' — 'hoc animo' is ablative of quality

steps.prompt: What type of ablative is 'hoc animo'?

hints.ablative of means: Ablative of means answers 'by what instrument' — 'hoc animo' describes Cicero's character
hints.ablative absolute: Ablative absolute requires a participle — 'hoc animo' is a descriptive ablative with 'fui'
hints.ablative of manner: Ablative of manner often uses 'cum' — 'hoc animo' describes an enduring quality, not manner of action

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'animi' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'animi' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'animi' ends in -i

hints.to become: 'To become' is 'fieri' — 'fui' is the perfect of 'esse' (to be)

steps.reveal: fui = I have been / I was

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'sum' — 'fui' is the perfect of 'esse'
hints.imperfect indicative: Imperfect would be 'eram' — 'fui' is perfect indicative
hints.pluperfect indicative: Pluperfect would be 'fueram' — 'fui' is perfect indicative

hints.fio, fieri, factus sum: These are the parts of 'fieri' (to become/be made) — 'fui' is from 'esse'
hints.fundo, fundere, fudi, fusum: These are the parts of 'fundere' (to pour) — 'fui' is from 'esse'
hints.fugio, fugere, fugi, fugitum: These are the parts of 'fugere' (to flee) — 'fui' is from 'esse'

steps.prompt: Why does Cicero use the emphatic 'Ego' when it is already implicit in the verb?
steps.target: to contrast himself emphatically with others in the debate
choices: to contrast himself emphatically with others in the debate
because Latin always requires a subject pronoun
to indicate a change of topic

hints.because Latin always requires a subject: Latin is a pro-drop language — subject pronouns are used for emphasis, not requirement
hints.to indicate a change of topic: Topic shifts don't require pronouns — 'Ego' here contrasts Cicero's resolve with Silanus's wavering
hints.it is a scribal addition: This is well-attested in the manuscript tradition — 'Ego' is Cicero's deliberate emphasis

steps.prompt: What does 'hoc' agree with in this sentence?

hints.'Ego' (nominative): 'Hoc' is ablative here matching 'animo' — if it modified 'Ego' it would be 'hic' (nom. masc.)
hints.'semper' (adverb): Adverbs cannot be modified by demonstrative pronouns — 'hoc' agrees with the noun 'animo'
hints.'fui' (verb): Demonstrative pronouns modify nouns, not verbs — 'hoc' agrees with 'animo'

translation: I have always been of this resolve
